title: Should you worry about high cholesterol? With Aseem Malhotra

chunks.title: Introduction to Cholesterol and Pharmaceutical Influence
chunks.summary: The podcast begins with a discussion on the fear surrounding cholesterol, emphasizing the financial motives of the pharmaceutical industry. The host introduces Dr. Asim Malhotra, a cardiologist who shares insights into cholesterol management and the misinformation prevalent in dietary guidelines.
chunks.transcript: we need to just take a step back and understand that the cholesterol lowering industry or the fear of cholesterol is a you know it's a trillion dollar business at least wow on this week's podcast I'm so grateful to have my friend asima hotra Dr Asim al-hotra he was recently on Joe Rogan's podcast and he did an amazing job there I hope you'll listen to that one as well in this podcast with the scene we talk about pharmaceuticals we talk about statins we talk about cholesterol he's a practicing cardiologist in the UK and so we talk about his views on LDL cholesterol whether it's a big deal that your cholesterol goes up a little bit when you're eating an animal-based diet or meat on any diet when you're having more saturated fat and less seed oils your LDL might go up is that bad he talks about how he thinks about risk from a cardiovascular perspective we talk about why so many people have a flawed perspective on LDL become myopic looking at LDL and we talk about what you really should be thinking of or what he and I really believe you should be thinking of when you're looking at your cholesterol and focusing on your metabolic Health we include tools including specific labs to assess your metabolic Health like fasting insulin or to assess your stress levels in an objective Manner and we talk about lifestyle interventions we also talk about a documentary that a seam is working on to expose the corruption in the pharmaceutical industry so I'm really grateful to have him on the podcast and I think you guys will really enjoy this one there is a ton of valuable information here Dr Aseem Malhotra thanks for coming on the

chunks.title: Dr. Asim Malhotra's Background and Medical Insight
chunks.summary: Dr. Malhotra shares his journey as a cardiologist and public health advocate. He discusses how his perspectives on medicine and the impact of diet on chronic diseases have evolved over his career, particularly in relation to the roles of big Pharma and processed foods.

chunks.title: Critique of Public Health Guidelines
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to criticize the dietary guidelines in both the UK and the US. The speakers argue that these guidelines are heavily influenced by the food industry, resulting in recommendations that contradict established health knowledge.

chunks.title: The Sugar Industry and Dietary Recommendations
chunks.summary: Discussion covers the historical influence of the sugar industry on dietary guidelines, detailing how marketing strategies shifted blame from sugar to fat. The speakers emphasize the need for public awareness regarding the manipulation of nutritional science.

chunks.title: The Role of Seed Oils and Dietary Health
chunks.summary: The effects of seed oils on health are explored, including their introduction into diets and the resultant increase in chronic diseases. The participants advocate for a return to traditional dietary practices, avoiding industrial seed oils.

chunks.title: Understanding Cholesterol and Cardiovascular Health
chunks.summary: Dr. Malhotra shares insights into cholesterol management, debunking common myths about LDL cholesterol as a sole indicator of heart health. He argues for focusing on metabolic health rather than merely lowering cholesterol numbers.
chunks.transcript: less saturated fat is is good so this is a crazy thing maybe we can dive into that because with your yes as a cardiologist I think this is really interesting this demonization of saturated fat absolutely so it's really interesting because it again comes back to cholesterol so I think people need to we need to just take a step back and understand that the cholesterol lowering industry or the fear of cholesterol is a you know it's a trillion dollar business at least wow you know we know that the total sales of statin drugs have been you know in the order of a trillion dollars and and that's extraordinary when you think about it so there's a huge industry around it there's food industry as well and what these seed oils do they do lower cholesterol but you know just to quote um a colleague of mine Professor Rita redberg who's a cardiologist at UCSF she's editor job internal medicine she says cholesterol just a lab number who cares about low in cholesterol unless it benefits a patient and when you look at the seed oils you're absolutely right Paul certainly when you um you know when they're heated uh and from cooking purposes we know they produce these toxic compounds called aldehydrates are carcinogenic um and uh and that's really really bad for the heart for the brain it's it's definitely linked to cancer as well um there is no real benefit at all it's only harmful to have seed oils in my view I don't think that you should be having seed oils at all they are pro-inflammatory and I think again with his distraction about lowering cholesterol was the primary way of preventing heart disease which has failed completely utterly miserably failed um it's distracted actually from the understanding now that heart disease is a chronic inflammatory condition which is exacerbated by metabolic risk factors so it had very little to do with cholesterol actually at all um the only reason we've got to this stage is because uh about one in 250 people suffer from a condition called familial type of epidemia and they have genetically very high levels of cholesterol and those people tend to develop heart disease compared to the normal population but develop heart disease versus the ones that don't have no difference in LDL it's probably something to do with those a subset of those people who have issues with clotting Etc so the whole cholesterol Paradigm needs to be really broken down and you know what I say to patients is I really I don't proactively by the way I've got to be honest here I'm a cardiologist and I do not proactively do anything to lower anyone's cholesterol at all at all because it's it's horseshit it is it is basically Ford science it's part of my language it's complete and we have shown that there is no correlation with prevention uh of reducing heart attacks and strokes and systematic review space medicine people like that up me and two other cardiologists we we showed that this hypothesis that at the lower your cholesterol the better is flawed um and and actually we can improve the cholesterol profile link to instant resistance and HDL and triglycerides for example but if we focus on insulin resistance so the cultural profile improves but total cholesterol and LDL

chunks.title: The Shadow of Statins and Pharmaceutical Influence
chunks.summary: The conversation turns to statin medications, discussing their widespread use and the potential side effects that are often minimized. The impact of pharmaceutical funding on medical research and guidelines is critically analyzed.

chunks.title: Lifestyle Modifications for Health Improvement
chunks.summary: Dr. Malhotra outlines lifestyle interventions for improving metabolic health, emphasizing diet, exercise, stress management, and sleep. He advocates for sustainable changes over reliance on medications.

chunks.title: Addressing Stress and Psychological Well-being
chunks.summary: The impact of chronic stress on health is discussed, highlighting its role in metabolic dysfunction and cardiovascular risk. The speakers suggest mindfulness and lifestyle adjustments as proactive solutions.

your life and really the you know medicine uh should be and this is well documented for read from researchers in this field I've been a big promoter of this is that actually giving in patients information in this way is actually the heart of ethical evidence-based Medical Practice it's people you need to give patients numbers in a way that they can understand which is free from coercion and manipulation of their hopes and anxieties and and that isn't done routinely and part of again Paul is not we weren't taught this at Medical School transparent communication of risk so I think that that highlights if you like

chunks.title: Announcement of Upcoming Documentary
chunks.summary: Dr. Malhotra introduces a crowdfunding initiative for a documentary titled 'First Do No Farm', aimed at exposing the corruption within Pharma and medicine. The film will highlight alternative approaches to health and wellness.
chunks.transcript: um what people should be really asking is that when a patient goes to their doctor you know it should be do I really need this drug what are the absolute benefits and what the
